exercises.title: Why you need one trace across the whole agent
exercises.prompt: An agent calls five tools, three MCP servers, and two sub-agents. Explain why you need one trace across all of it, why OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions are the 2026 standard, and the span hierarchy (agent → LLM → tool).
exercises.hint: Without one trace you can't see what the agent actually did across tools/servers/sub-agents — debugging is blind. OTel GenAI conventions = standard attributes natively supported by Datadog, Langfuse, Phoenix, etc. Span hierarchy: agent loop span → LLM call spans → tool call spans, nested.
exercises.solution:
**Why you need one trace across the whole agent:** a real agent invocation **fans out across many components** — five tool calls, three MCP servers, two sub-agents (A2A delegations, Lesson 19), multiple LLM calls in the loop. When something goes wrong (a wrong answer, a slow response, a failed tool, a cost spike), you need to **see the *entire* execution as one connected picture** — which LLM call decided to call which tool, which MCP server handled it, how long each took, what each returned, where it failed. Without **one trace spanning all of it**, you're **debugging blind**: you have scattered logs from different components with no way to connect them into 'what did this agent invocation actually do, step by step?' Agents are **distributed systems** (across tools/servers/sub-agents), so they need **distributed tracing** — one trace tying every hop together.

**Why OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions are the 2026 standard:** **OpenTelemetry (OTel)** is the **vendor-neutral observability standard**, and its **GenAI semantic conventions** (stable attributes in v1.37+) define **standard attribute names and span structures for LLM/agent observability** — so that an LLM span, a tool span, etc., have **consistent, agreed-upon attributes** regardless of who emits them. They're the 2026 standard because:
- **Native support across the ecosystem:** **Datadog, Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, OpenLLMetry, AgentOps** (and more) **natively support** the OTel GenAI conventions — so if you emit OTel-conformant spans, **any of these backends can ingest and visualize them** without custom adapters. Write once, observe anywhere (the same standardization benefit as MCP for tools).
- **Vendor-neutral / no lock-in:** you instrument with OTel and can **swap observability backends** (Jaeger, Langfuse, Datadog) without re-instrumenting — you're not locked to one vendor's SDK.
- **Standardized attributes** mean **comparable, queryable telemetry** across tools, teams, and vendors.
It's the 'one standard so everything interoperates' pattern, applied to agent observability.

**The span hierarchy (agent → LLM → tool):** traces are organized as **nested spans** mirroring the agent's structure:
```
agent-loop span                          (the whole invocation)
  ├── LLM-call span                      (model decides what to do)
  ├── tool-call span                     (a tool executes)
  │     └── MCP-client-dispatch span     (routing to the MCP server)
  ├── LLM-call span                      (model observes, decides next)
  ├── tool-call span
  └── sub-agent span (A2A)               (delegated work)
```
- The **agent-loop span** is the **root** — the whole agent invocation.
- **LLM-call spans** are children — each model call (decide step, Lesson 1).
- **tool-call spans** are children — each tool execution (and may nest **MCP-client-dispatch** spans for the routing to servers).
- **sub-agent spans** for A2A delegations.
The **nesting captures causality and timing** — you see that *this* LLM call triggered *that* tool call, which took *this* long and called *that* MCP server. The hierarchy reconstructs the **agent → LLM → tool** flow (the four-step loop, Lesson 1) as a navigable trace. This is what turns 'the agent did something' into 'here's exactly what it did, in order, with timings and results' — the foundation of debugging, cost-tracking, and performance analysis for agents (Phase 11.13's observability, made standard).

exercises.title: Required OTel GenAI attributes and a span emitter
exercises.prompt: Name the required OTel GenAI attributes for an LLM span and a tool-execution span, build a trace hierarchy (agent → LLM → tool → MCP dispatch), and implement a stdlib span emitter. Explain how to emit spans without rewriting tool code.
exercises.hint: LLM span: gen_ai.system, gen_ai.request.model, token usage, etc. Tool span: gen_ai.tool.name, tool.call.id, arguments/results (opt-in). Emit spans by wrapping the loop/dispatch with span context (decorators/context managers) — no tool-logic changes. Export to a collector.
exercises.solution:
**Required OTel GenAI attributes:**
- **LLM span** (a model call): `gen_ai.system` (the provider — openai/anthropic/etc.), `gen_ai.request.model` (the model id), `gen_ai.operation.name` (e.g. chat), **token usage** (`gen_ai.usage.input_tokens`, `gen_ai.usage.output_tokens`), request parameters (temperature, etc.), and **opt-in** content (prompt/completion). These capture **what model was called, how, and at what token cost** (essential for cost tracking, Phase 11.11).
- **Tool-execution span** (a tool call): `gen_ai.tool.name` (which tool), `gen_ai.tool.call.id` (correlating to the model's tool-call id, Lesson 3), the **tool type**, and **opt-in** arguments/results. Plus standard span fields (start/end time → duration, status/error). These capture **which tool ran, how long it took, and whether it succeeded**.
Both share standard span attributes (name, timing, status, parent span id for nesting).

**A stdlib span emitter + trace hierarchy:**
```python
import time, uuid, contextlib

class Tracer:
    def __init__(self, exporter): self.exporter = exporter; self.stack = []
    @contextlib.contextmanager
    def span(self, name, attributes=None):
        span = {"name": name, "span_id": uuid.uuid4().hex,
                "parent_id": self.stack[-1]["span_id"] if self.stack else None,
                "start": time.time(), "attributes": attributes or {}}
        self.stack.append(span)
        try:
            yield span
            span["status"] = "ok"
        except Exception as e:
            span["status"] = "error"; span["error"] = str(e); raise
        finally:
            span["end"] = time.time(); self.stack.pop()
            self.exporter.export(span)        # send to collector (Jaeger/Langfuse)

# Build the hierarchy by nesting span context managers:
with tracer.span("agent.invoke"):                          # root: agent loop
    with tracer.span("gen_ai.chat", {"gen_ai.system": "anthropic",
                                     "gen_ai.request.model": "claude"}):
        ...  # LLM call (emits token usage)
    with tracer.span("gen_ai.tool", {"gen_ai.tool.name": "get_weather"}):
        with tracer.span("mcp.dispatch", {"mcp.server": "weather-server"}):
            ...  # MCP client routes to the server
```
The **nesting** (via the span stack tracking `parent_id`) builds the **agent → LLM → tool → MCP-dispatch hierarchy** automatically — each `span()` context manager becomes a child of the currently-open span. On exit, each span records duration and status and is **exported to a collector** (Jaeger, Langfuse) for visualization.

**How to emit spans without rewriting tool code:** the key is **instrument the loop/dispatch layer, not the tools**:
- **Wrap the agent loop, LLM calls, and the tool *dispatch*** (Lesson 7's dispatch loop) with span context managers — so spans are created **around** tool execution, not inside each tool's logic.
- Use **decorators or context managers** at the **dispatcher** so **every tool call gets a span automatically** — the tool's pure-function logic (Lesson 7) is **unchanged**; the dispatcher that calls it adds the span.
- This works because the **tools are pure functions separated from plumbing** (Lesson 7) — observability is **plumbing**, so it lives in the dispatch/loop layer, wrapping the tools without touching them. (Many SDKs auto-instrument — you just enable OTel and the LLM/MCP calls emit spans.)
So you get **full tracing for free** at the orchestration layer — the same payoff as separating logic from transport (Lesson 7): you can **add observability (and swap exporters) without rewriting tool logic**. Emit the standard OTel GenAI attributes (system, model, tokens, tool name, timing, status) at the loop/LLM/tool/dispatch boundaries, and any OTel backend visualizes the full agent trace.

exercises.title: Content capture vs redaction, and observability as the production-agent foundation
exercises.prompt: Explain the decision of what content to capture (opt-in) vs redact (defaults), why this matters for privacy/security, and how OTel GenAI tracing underpins production agent reliability (debugging, cost, latency, regressions) across the whole tool ecosystem (MCP, A2A, gateways). Why is standardized tracing essential as agents grow complex?
exercises.hint: Capture: span structure, timings, tokens, tool names always; prompt/completion CONTENT is opt-in (default redact) because it may contain PII/secrets. Tracing enables: debug (see the trace), cost (token attrs), latency (span durations), regression detection. Standard = works across MCP/A2A/gateways/vendors. Essential because agents are complex distributed systems.
exercises.solution:
**What to capture (opt-in) vs redact (defaults):**
- **Always safe to capture (default on):** **span structure and metadata** — span names, the hierarchy (parent/child), **timings/durations**, **status/errors**, **token usage** (`gen_ai.usage.*`), **model and provider** (`gen_ai.system`, `gen_ai.request.model`), **tool names**. These are **operational metadata** with no sensitive content — they tell you *what happened, how long, how much it cost, and whether it failed* without exposing data.
- **Opt-in (default REDACTED):** the **actual content** — **prompts, completions, tool arguments, tool results**. The OTel GenAI conventions make **content capture opt-in** (off by default) because **this content frequently contains sensitive data** — **PII, secrets, credentials, proprietary information, user data**. Capturing it by default and shipping it to an observability backend would be a **privacy/security disaster** (leaking PII/secrets into logs/traces, possibly to a third-party vendor). So content is **redacted by default** and only captured when **explicitly enabled** (and even then, often with PII-scrubbing).

**Why this matters for privacy/security:** traces flow to **observability backends** (possibly third-party SaaS like Datadog), are **stored and queryable**, and may be **accessed by many people**. If prompts/completions/tool-args contain **user PII, passwords, API keys, medical/financial data**, capturing them by default **leaks sensitive data into the observability pipeline** — a compliance violation (GDPR/HIPAA) and a security risk (secrets in logs are a classic breach vector). The **default-redact, opt-in-capture** policy ensures you get **operational visibility (timings, tokens, structure, errors) without leaking content** — and when you *do* need content (deep debugging), you **opt in deliberately, with scrubbing**. It's the **privacy-by-default** principle applied to observability — the same care as not putting PII in URLs or logs.

**How OTel GenAI tracing underpins production agent reliability:** standardized tracing is the **observability foundation** for production agents (Phase 11.13), enabling:
- **Debugging:** when an agent gives a wrong/odd answer, the **trace shows the full execution** — which LLM call decided what, which tools ran with what results, where it failed. You can **reconstruct exactly what happened** (essential because agent behavior is non-deterministic and non-reproducible without the trace, Phase 11.10).
- **Cost tracking:** **token-usage attributes per LLM span** let you compute **cost per invocation, per tool, per user** (Phase 11.11's cost economics) — find what's expensive, set budgets.
- **Latency analysis:** **span durations** reveal **where time goes** — a slow tool, a slow MCP server, a slow sub-agent (A2A) — so you can optimize (parallelize, Lesson 3; cache; route).
- **Regression detection:** track trace metrics (latency, cost, error rate, tool-selection patterns) **over time** to **catch regressions** when prompts/models/tools change (Phase 11.10's regression dashboards).
- **Error/failure analysis:** span statuses surface **which tools/servers fail and how often**.

**Across the whole tool ecosystem (MCP, A2A, gateways):** because OTel is **standardized and vendor-neutral**, **one trace spans the entire heterogeneous stack** — the agent loop, LLM calls, **MCP tool calls (Lessons 6-14)**, **A2A sub-agent delegations (Lesson 19)**, and **gateway hops (Lesson 17)** — all emitting **conformant spans** that **stitch into one trace**. The gateway can add spans (audit + tracing), MCP clients/servers emit spans, A2A delegations propagate trace context — so you see **end-to-end across tools, servers, sub-agents, and the gateway**. Standardization is what makes this possible: without a common convention, each component's telemetry would be incompatible and you couldn't build one trace.

**Why standardized tracing is essential as agents grow complex:** as agents get more complex — **many tools, multiple MCP servers, gateways, A2A sub-agents, long-running tasks, parallel calls** — they become **deeply distributed systems**, and **complexity makes them opaque and hard to debug/operate** without observability. Like any distributed system, you **cannot operate what you cannot see**. And because agents **fail silently and non-deterministically** (Phase 11.10 — fluent wrong answers, no crash), tracing is the **only way to know what an agent actually did** and whether it's healthy (correct, fast, cost-effective). **Standardized** tracing (OTel GenAI) is essential specifically because the ecosystem is **heterogeneous** (many vendors, protocols, frameworks) — only a common standard lets you trace across MCP + A2A + gateways + multiple LLM providers + multiple observability backends **coherently**. The synthesis: as agents scale into complex, distributed, multi-protocol systems, **standardized, privacy-respecting observability (OTel GenAI: capture structure/timings/tokens, redact content by default) is the non-negotiable foundation** for debugging, cost control, latency optimization, and regression detection — the operational backbone that makes production agents *operable* (Phase 11.13's lesson, now standardized across the entire tools-and-protocols stack).
